ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general paratha entry (FOODON_03302397); no specific Warki/layered variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific warki paratha code. A00LC (unleavened bread/flatbread) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code exists for warki paratha or layered Indian flatbread. None assigned.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium warki paratha piece weighing approximately 80 g, as typically served at an Indian restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Paratha (plain, cooked) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports plain paratha (cooked) per 100 g. Warki paratha is a laminated, multi-layered variant made with additional ghee/oil for lamination and ajwain seasoning. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain paratha baseline with a modest upward adjustment to fat and energy to reflect the lamination fat content typical of warki paratha, consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for layered flatbreads. Protein and carbohydrate values are held close to the ICMR-NIN plain paratha reference.

gi_source.title: Chapati / Paratha GI reference — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Diabetes Care
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for warki paratha specifically. The University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) report GI values for Indian wheat flatbreads (chapati/paratha) in the range of 55–70, with plain paratha typically cited at approximately 62 (medium GI). Warki paratha uses similar wheat flour base; the lamination fat may marginally lower GI but the effect is modest. GI of 62 is used as the best analogous estimate from the approved GI source. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (62 × 37.2) / 100 ≈ 23.1, rounded to 23.0. Serving carbohydrate = 46.5 g/100g × 80 g serving = 37.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g) and use of ghee in lamination
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit warki paratha to one piece per meal and avoid additional high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or salted chutneys, as the combined sodium load can approach or exceed 400 mg per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~62) and moderate glycemic load (~23 per 80 g serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume warki paratha in controlled portions (one piece) and pair it with high-fibre vegetables or legumes to blunt the postprandial glycaemic response, given its medium GI of approximately 62 and glycemic load of approximately 23 per serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee lamination (~4.8 g/100 g) and refined flour component
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should moderate warki paratha intake due to its saturated fat content from ghee used in lamination; choosing versions made with unsaturated oil instead of ghee and limiting to one piece per meal is advisable to keep saturated fat within daily targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Warki Paratha is a traditional North Indian multi-layered flatbread distinguished by its flaky, laminated texture achieved through a technique of folding and rolling dough with ghee or oil to create multiple thin, crisp layers — the word 'warki' (or 'varqi') means 'layered' or 'leaf-like' in Urdu and Hindi. It is flavoured with ajwain (carom seeds), which impart a distinctive pungent, thyme-like aroma and are traditionally believed to aid digestion. The bread is baked in a tandoor (clay oven) at high heat, producing a slightly charred exterior with a soft, flaky interior. Warki paratha is associated with Mughal and North Indian culinary traditions and is commonly served as an accompaniment to rich curries, dal, or kebabs. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (~326 kcal/100 g) primarily from carbohydrates (~46.5 g/100 g) and fat (~12.5 g/100 g), with a modest protein contribution (~7.8 g/100 g). The fat content is higher than plain paratha due to the lamination process. It has a medium glycemic index (~62) and a glycemic load of approximately 23 per 80 g serving, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The sodium content (~310 mg/100 g) warrants attention for those with hypertension. Ajwain seeds contribute trace iron and are a source of thymol, a bioactive compound with antimicrobial properties. In Singapore, warki paratha is served at select Indian restaurants as a premium bread option alongside North Indian and Mughal-style dishes.
common_user_queries: How many calories in warki paratha?
What is the difference between warki paratha and plain paratha?
Is warki paratha good for diabetics?
What does warki mean in Indian food?
Is warki paratha the same as flaky paratha?
meal_context: accompaniment to dal or lentil dishes
served with kebabs or grilled meats

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry for warki paratha exists; values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain paratha with fat/energy adjustment for lamination. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for Indian wheat flatbreads. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source provides a direct warki paratha nutrient profile.
notes: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain paratha (cooked) per 100 g, with fat and energy adjusted upward to reflect the additional ghee/oil used in warki paratha lamination. Vitamin A is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN paratha entry for this preparation. Vitamin B6 is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food. Vitamin B12 is -1 as this is a plant-based bread product and B12 is not present; however, the value is -1 (not reported) rather than 0 to follow the sentinel convention. Folate is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN paratha entry. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food. Vitamin C is 0.0 — confirmed zero as wheat flatbreads contain no ascorbic acid; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy for cooked wheat flatbreads. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for Indian wheat flatbreads (paratha/chapati range). Glycemic load per 80 g serving = (62 × 37.2) / 100 ≈ 23.0 g. The food is served at Indian House/Dumpukht Grills & Curry, Boat Quay, Singapore (field survey source). Ontology codes use closest_parent as no exact warki paratha code exists in FoodOn or FoodEx2.
